title: The Switch

jokes.text: Tennis is the only sport where the uniform is what you'd wear under your clothes in any other sport. You're actually out there in your underwear.
jokes.setup: Tennis uniforms look like regular sportswear
jokes.punchline: They're essentially underwear compared to other sports
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational premise with good economy - classic Jerry opener that reframes something familiar in an absurd way

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Tennis uniforms look like regular sportswear, but they're essentially underwear compared to other sports.

jokes.text: That's why they started keeping score like that. Point? You know what? Make it 15. I'm dying in this sweater here. Another one got by me? Take another 10 points. Let's just get this over with.
jokes.setup: Tennis scoring system is bizarre (15-30-40)
jokes.punchline: Players just wanted to end matches quickly due to uncomfortable clothing
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist explanation for tennis scoring - original premise with good escalation and historical 'logic'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, I think I've figured out why tennis scoring is so weird. 15, 30, 40... it makes no sense, right? But here's the thing - back in the day, players were wearing these incredibly uncomfortable clothes. Tight pants, heavy shirts, the whole deal. So they just wanted to end the matches as fast as possible. The scoring system? That was basically the players going, 'Look, we don't care about logic here. We just need to get off this court before we pass out from heat exhaustion.'

jokes.text: Really? Grace? / Yes.
jokes.setup: Elaine says she's not very good at tennis
jokes.punchline: She's surprised someone thinks she has grace
jokes.explanation: Mild character moment showing Elaine's self-deprecation, but fairly standard

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'm not very good at tennis.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? You have such grace on the court.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Grace? Me?

jokes.text: Oh, Bruline. Newman's got the same one. / Newman plays tennis? / He's fantastic.
jokes.setup: Newman is established as an unlikely athlete
jokes.punchline: Revelation that Newman is fantastic at tennis
jokes.explanation: Good character-based surprise - Newman being athletic contradicts his established persona

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Newman plays tennis?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: He's actually really good.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Newman? At tennis?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: He's won three tournaments this year.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Newman...

jokes.text: Next time let's play Ping-Pong. It's easier to jump over the net.
jokes.setup: Tennis nets are regulation height
jokes.punchline: Jerry suggests ping-pong because he can jump over that net
jokes.explanation: Simple visual gag with decent economy but not particularly original

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, if tennis nets are regulation height, maybe we should just play ping-pong. I can jump over that net.

jokes.text: Have you noticed that she never laughs? / Really? / Yeah. Think about it.
jokes.setup: Jerry brings up his girlfriend's lack of laughter
jokes.punchline: George realizes it's true when prompted to think
jokes.explanation: Sets up major plot point about non-laughing girlfriend, solid character observation

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: She never laughs. I don't think I've ever heard her laugh.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's not true.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, it's true. Think about it.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You're right. She's never laughed.

jokes.text: George, you're becoming one of the glitterati. / What's that? / You know, people who glitter.
jokes.setup: George is dating an attractive model
jokes.punchline: Jerry's mock-serious definition of glitterati
jokes.explanation: Simple wordplay with Jerry's characteristic deadpan delivery

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what the glitterati are? They're people who have glitter on them. That's it. That's the whole thing. You go to a party, there's some glitter on your jacket, boom, you're glitterati.

jokes.text: Maybe she's bulimic. / What? / Bulimic, you know? / Kramer, she's a model. / Exactly.
jokes.setup: Discussion of model's large appetite despite being thin
jokes.punchline: Kramer's matter-of-fact suggestion and 'Exactly' response
jokes.explanation: Dark humor delivered with Kramer's characteristic bluntness - good character integration

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You gotta eat. Look at you, you're wasting away.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I eat!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No, you gotta really eat. You know, consume mass quantities.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Exactly.

jokes.text: There you go, monkey boy. Come on.
jokes.setup: Kramer has convinced George about bulimia possibility
jokes.punchline: Kramer's casual 'monkey boy' nickname for George
jokes.explanation: Typical Kramer random nickname delivery - economical and character-specific

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You gotta see a doctor, monkey boy.

jokes.text: Frankenstein didn't seem quite right to me. I missed the sport jacket. Not that it was that nice of a jacket. I mean, it didn't fit him that well. To me there's just something about a monster in a blazer. It shows at least he's making an effort.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Frankenstein movie
jokes.punchline: George's detailed analysis of monster's fashion choices showing 'effort'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist observation - George's petty focus on fashion in horror context is perfectly in character and original

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about this Frankenstein thing. The monster, he's wearing that bolted neck collar, right? That's not just thrown together. That's a statement. That's effort. He's got the flat-top hairdo, the stitched-up face—these are deliberate choices. The guy's put thought into his appearance. He's not just stumbling around; he's got a whole look going.

jokes.text: That's funny.
jokes.setup: George tells elaborate joke about Frankenstein's blazer
jokes.punchline: Nina says 'That's funny' instead of laughing
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback and setup for running gag - economical and character-defining moment

dialogue.speaker: Nina
dialogue.line: That's funny.

jokes.text: I'm so full. / Yes. Full. Love to be full. Love to just sit back, loosen the old belt and digest away for hours. Let those enzymes do their work.
jokes.setup: Nina mentions being full after eating
jokes.punchline: George's overly enthusiastic, detailed response about digestion
jokes.explanation: Classic George over-sharing cringe moment - well-integrated with his paranoia about bulimia

dialogue.speaker: Nina
dialogue.line: I'm so full, I couldn't eat another bite.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Oh, that's great! You know, the human stomach can hold about two to four liters. But once food enters the small intestine, that's where the real magic happens. The chyme — that's partially digested food, by the way — it moves through there at about one centimeter per second. Fascinating stuff, really.

jokes.text: Where are you going? / I just need to freshen up. / You're fresh. You're very fresh. You seem very fresh to me. You're very vital. I couldn't take you any fresher.
jokes.setup: Nina wants to use the bathroom
jokes.punchline: George's desperate, repetitive attempts to keep her from going
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating cringe - George's desperation builds perfectly with each 'fresh' repetition

dialogue.speaker: Nina
dialogue.line: I need to use the bathroom.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, no, no! You can't go in there right now.

dialogue.speaker: Nina
dialogue.line: What? Why not?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Because... because I'm using it. I'm in the middle of something.

dialogue.speaker: Nina
dialogue.line: You're not in the bathroom.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I will be! Any second now. I'm about to go in there. You gotta wait.

dialogue.speaker: Nina
dialogue.line: George, I really need to—

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No! Not now. Later. Use it later.

dialogue.speaker: Nina
dialogue.line: This is ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Just... just give me five minutes. No, ten. Ten minutes and it's all yours.

jokes.text: The jokes kept bouncing off her like Superman.
jokes.setup: Jerry describing his girlfriend's lack of reaction to humor
jokes.punchline: Comparing jokes bouncing off her to Superman deflecting bullets
jokes.explanation: Great visual metaphor with perfect economy - Superman comparison is vivid and quotable

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: She just sits there. I'm telling jokes and they're just bouncing off her like I'm Superman and she's a bullet.

jokes.text: Even when she did like something... she doesn't laugh. She says, 'That's funny.'
jokes.setup: Continuation of non-laughing girlfriend discussion
jokes.punchline: Jerry mimicking her saying 'That's funny' instead of laughing
jokes.explanation: Reinforces the running gag and makes it explicit - sets up future callbacks

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: She doesn't laugh. She just says 'That's funny.' 'That's funny.' No laugh, just the words. 'That's funny.'

jokes.text: How could I be with someone that doesn't laugh? It's like.... Well, it's like something.
jokes.setup: Jerry struggling to articulate his dilemma
jokes.punchline: His inability to complete the analogy
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Jerry's verbal limitations when truly frustrated

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's like... it's like when you're trying to explain something but you can't find the right words, you know? It's like... it's like a... a what?

jokes.text: Well, I heard a noise. / What noise? / You know:
jokes.setup: George explains his bathroom surveillance
jokes.punchline: George making vomiting sound effects
jokes.explanation: Physical cringe humor - George's willingness to make the sound is in character but not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, I set up a camera in there. I got it all on tape.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, what are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What? I'm documenting everything. You never know when you're gonna need evidence.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: *making vomiting sounds* Bleeeegh! Huurrrgh!

jokes.text: Elaine, of course I'm concerned. I'm paying for those meals. It's like throwing money down the toilet. / In a manner of speaking.
jokes.setup: George worried about bulimia for financial reasons
jokes.punchline: Elaine's 'manner of speaking' acknowledgment of the toilet metaphor
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with decent wordplay - George's selfishness is perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Do you know how much money I've been spending on food? If she's just going to throw it up, that's like throwing money directly into the toilet.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, in a manner of speaking...

jokes.text: What would be good... is if there was someone else in the bathroom that could tell me.
jokes.setup: George needs verification of bulimia theory
jokes.punchline: His matter-of-fact suggestion for bathroom surveillance
jokes.explanation: Absurdist escalation of George's paranoia - perfectly captures his willingness to cross boundaries

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: We need to put a camera in the bathroom.

jokes.text: My mother's a matron! / Babs? / Yeah, there. All right? I said it. You satisfied? Anything else you wanna know?
jokes.setup: Kramer's reluctance to discuss his mother
jokes.punchline: His explosive confession about Babs being a bathroom matron
jokes.explanation: Good character revelation with emotional stakes - Kramer's shame is both funny and humanizing

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So what's the big deal? Just tell me about your mother.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I don't want to talk about it.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Come on, Kramer. How bad could it be?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: My mother was a bathroom matron!

jokes.text: I'm still carrying a lot of pain. A lot of pain.
jokes.setup: Kramer explaining his family issues
jokes.punchline: His over-serious repetition of 'a lot of pain'
jokes.explanation: Character moment with mild humor from Kramer's mock-serious delivery

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: My father, he didn't really give me a lot of guidance. You know, I was very much a latchkey kid. And, uh, my mother, she was very busy with her life. So I didn't really have anybody to talk to. So there was a lot of pain. There was a lot of pain.

jokes.text: I tore my humeral epicondylitis.
jokes.setup: Tennis injury explanation
jokes.punchline: Using medical terminology for tennis elbow
jokes.explanation: Mild humor from pretentious medical jargon for common condition

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What happened to your arm?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Tennis elbow. I've been playing a lot of tennis.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Tennis elbow? That's what they call it?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Yeah, lateral epicondylitis. It's the medical term.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Lateral epicondylitis... and here I thought you just played badly.

jokes.text: There are plenty of things you can do. There's chess... and mahjong.
jokes.setup: Elaine trying to console injured tennis player
jokes.punchline: Her awkward suggestion of sedentary activities
jokes.explanation: Mild humor from Elaine's social awkwardness and poor consolation attempts

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You know, there's a lot of things you can do while you're laid up. You could read books, watch TV, listen to music...

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You could learn a language! I've always wanted to learn Italian.

jokes.text: If I can't play tennis, I have no reason to live.
jokes.setup: Tennis injury preventing play
jokes.punchline: Dramatic overreaction about having no reason to live
jokes.explanation: Standard dramatic overreaction humor - creates awkward moment for Elaine

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: A tennis injury? That's it, I have no reason to live.

jokes.text: Ma? / Cosmo.
jokes.setup: Kramer calls out to his mother
jokes.punchline: She responds with his real first name
jokes.explanation: Economical setup for major revelation - builds suspense perfectly with minimal dialogue

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Mom!

dialogue.speaker: Babs
dialogue.line: Cosmo!

jokes.text: Cosmo?
jokes.setup: George hears Kramer's real name for first time
jokes.punchline: His confused repetition
jokes.explanation: Simple reaction beat that builds to bigger revelation

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Cosmo Kramer.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Cosmo?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Cosmo Kramer.

jokes.text: He needs a $300 Bruline to beat Ethel Kennedy?
jokes.setup: Mr. Pitt's expensive tennis racket requirement
jokes.punchline: Questioning need for expensive equipment against elderly Kennedy
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about sports equipment pretension and elderly opponents

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you need an expensive tennis racket to play tennis?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Pitt
dialogue.line: Of course! You can't play tennis without the proper equipment.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Really? Because I'm pretty sure Jackie Kennedy played tennis, and she used the same racket for like thirty years.

jokes.text: And while I was there, I happened to pick up... another juicy little nugget about our friend.
jokes.setup: George teasing big revelation about Kramer
jokes.punchline: His dramatic buildup for the name reveal
jokes.explanation: Good buildup creating suspense - George's dramatic storytelling style

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. You're not going to believe what I just found out about Kramer.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: His first name... it's not what you think.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You ready for this?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: His name is... Cosmo.

jokes.text: Cosmo. / Cosmo? / Cosmo? / Cosmo. / Cosmo?
jokes.setup: George reveals Kramer's first name
jokes.punchline: Everyone's repeated incredulous reactions
jokes.explanation: Effective group reaction building the absurdity of the name through repetition

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: His name is Cosmo!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Cosmo?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Cosmo?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Cosmo Kramer.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Cosmo?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I never knew that.

jokes.text: Cosmo!
jokes.setup: The name revelation sinking in
jokes.punchline: Jerry's emphatic exclamation
jokes.explanation: Caps off the revelation sequence with Jerry's characteristic emphasis

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Her name is Mulva!

jokes.text: You did a hell of a job keeping it a secret all these years.
jokes.setup: Kramer's name was unknown for years
jokes.punchline: Jerry acknowledging the successful secret-keeping
jokes.explanation: Meta-humor acknowledging the long-running mystery in the show

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I can't believe we kept Kramer's first name a secret for nine years.

jokes.text: All this time I'm trying not to be me. I'm afraid to face who I was. But I'm Cosmo, Jerry. I'm Cosmo Kramer, and that's who I'm gonna be. From now on, I'm Cosmo.
jokes.setup: Kramer's identity crisis about his name
jokes.punchline: His dramatic declaration of self-acceptance
jokes.explanation: Character development moment with humor from Kramer's over-serious tone about a name

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: My name is Cosmo Kramer. I'm the Kramer. The one and only.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I don't need to change who I am. I am who I am, and that's enough.

jokes.text: Well, I would except I forgot to bring a towel.
jokes.setup: Roommate invites Jerry in while girlfriend showers
jokes.punchline: Jerry's excuse about needing a towel (implying orgy)
jokes.explanation: Clever sexual innuendo disguised as innocent practicality - economical and quotable

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I need a towel.

dialogue.speaker: Roommate
dialogue.line: What for?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm gonna take a shower.

dialogue.speaker: Roommate
dialogue.line: You just took a shower at your place.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm a very clean person.

jokes.text: None of those big, coarse 'ha's.' / Oh, yeah. / Hate the big, coarse 'ha.' I hate those.
jokes.setup: Jerry describing attractive laugh qualities
jokes.punchline: George's emphatic agreement about hating coarse laughs
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about laugh quality with nice character agreement

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: There's nothing worse than a coarse laugh. You know what I mean? A real grating, annoying laugh.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Oh, I hate that! I hate a coarse laugh!

jokes.text: Do you realize in the entire history of Western civilization... no one successfully accomplished the roommate switch. In the Middle Ages you could get locked up for even suggesting it.
jokes.setup: Discussion of switching girlfriends with roommate
jokes.punchline: Jerry's mock-historical analysis of the 'roommate switch'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist escalation - treating dating scenario like historical impossibility is very original

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, historians will look back on this moment. 'The Great Roommate Switch of '95.' They'll write papers about it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Sociologists will study the implications. 'How did this happen? What were they thinking?'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And the answer will be: they weren't.

jokes.text: They didn't have roommates in the Middle Ages. / How do you know? / For one thing... they didn't have apartments.
jokes.setup: George's logical objection to medieval roommates
jokes.punchline: Jerry's matter-of-fact explanation about apartments
jokes.explanation: Good logical follow-up with Jerry's deadpan historical fact

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You can't have medieval people as roommates. They'll want to use a catapult in the living room.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, that's what happens when you get an apartment in New York. You take what you can get.

jokes.text: I'm sure at some point between the years 800 and 1200 somewhere... there were two women living together.
jokes.setup: Defending the medieval roommate theory
jokes.punchline: Jerry's specific date range for hypothetical roommates
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of absurd historical premise with pseudo-scholarly specificity

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm saying, if I had a roommate, it would have to be from medieval times. Like, 1200 to 1400. That's it. Those are the only centuries I'm comfortable sharing an apartment with someone from.

jokes.text: So if you're scared... if you haven't got the stomach for this, let's get it out right now. And I'll go on my own. If not, you can get onboard, and we can get to work. Now, what's it gonna be? / All right, damn it, I'm in.
jokes.setup: Jerry's dramatic challenge about the roommate switch
jokes.punchline: George's dramatic acceptance like a war movie
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation treating trivial dating issue like military operation - great character work

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what you're saying is, you want to switch roommates with me?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's right. I'm going in.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, this is a big decision. Once you go through that door, there's no turning back.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I know what I'm doing, Jerry. I've thought about this long and hard. Tell my mother... tell her I love her.

jokes.text: We can't do it. Who are we kidding? It's impossible. It's true. You can't do the switch. Nobody can do the switch.
jokes.setup: George and Jerry planning the roommate switch
jokes.punchline: George's defeatist spiral about the impossibility
jokes.explanation: Classic George defeatism - perfectly in character pessimistic spiral

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It'll never work. She'll take one look at me and realize I'm a complete loser.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You don't know that.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I know it. Women can sense these things. They have radar for failure. I walk in that apartment, she's gonna take one whiff and know I've spent the last fifteen years doing nothing.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So don't tell her that.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I won't have to tell her. It's written all over my face. It's in the way I stand, the way I sit. She'll know within thirty seconds that I'm a guy who gets dropped off at work by his mother.

jokes.text: I got it!
jokes.setup: Jerry and George give up on the switch plan
jokes.punchline: Jerry's sudden eureka moment after apparent defeat
jokes.explanation: Classic plot device with good timing - simple but effective structure

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's not going to work.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Because it's stupid. That's why.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You got a better idea?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Then we do it.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I don't know...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. I just thought of something.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What if I just walked up to her and told her the truth?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: The truth?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I like you. You're a good-looking woman. I'd like to go out with you sometime. Just like that.

jokes.text: So I tell Sandi that I wanna have a ménage à trois... with her and her roommate.
jokes.setup: Jerry's plan to switch girlfriends
jokes.punchline: His solution is to suggest a threesome
jokes.explanation: Subversive solution to dating problem - original approach that's shocking but logical

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what? I got it. I'll just suggest a threesome. That way, nobody feels left out, and I get to see two women at the same time. It's the perfect solution.

jokes.text: Firstly, the very mention of the idea will cause Sandi to recoil in disgust. Whereupon, she will insist that I remove myself from the premises.
jokes.setup: George explaining Jerry's ménage à trois plan
jokes.punchline: His overly formal, academic language for dating scheme
jokes.explanation: Excellent character work - George's pretentious vocabulary elevates silly scheme

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: He's proposing a ménage à trois arrangement. It's actually quite brilliant when you think about it from a strategic standpoint. You see, the traditional monogamous dating paradigm has inherent inefficiencies. By introducing a tertiary romantic component, you create a kind of emotional redundancy system.

jokes.text: There's only one flaw in it. They're roommates. She'd have to go out with me behind Sandi's back. She's not gonna do that. / You disappoint me, my friend.
jokes.setup: George finds logical flaw in the plan
jokes.punchline: Jerry's dramatic disappointment response
jokes.explanation: Good character interplay with Jerry's mock-serious disappointment

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. This plan has a logical flaw.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What? No it doesn't!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yes it does. You're telling me that...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh, come on!

jokes.text: She tells Laura, 'If you wanna waste your time with that pervert... that's your problem.'
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining how the plan will work
jokes.punchline: His prediction of being called a pervert
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the plan with Jerry accepting the 'pervert' label

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And that's when they're going to call me a pervert.

jokes.text: It's the perfect plan. So inspired, so devious, yet so simple. This is what I do.
jokes.setup: Jerry has worked out the roommate switch plan
jokes.punchline: His grandiose self-congratulation
jokes.explanation: Character moment showing Jerry's pride in scheming - self-aggrandizing humor

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm a genius. A pure genius. I have solved the roommate problem. I am the roommate problem solver. There should be a statue of me in the town square.

jokes.text: No. I'm okay. / Then what are you doing... with that racket? / It's mine. / Ms. Landis borrowed it.
jokes.setup: Elaine caught with tennis racket in office
jokes.punchline: Her awkward attempt to explain ownership
jokes.explanation: Standard cringe scenario - Elaine caught in awkward situation

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: This? Oh, this is... this is my tennis racket.

dialogue.speaker: Assistant
dialogue.line: In the office?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Yes, well, I was going to play tennis. After work. So I brought it in.

dialogue.speaker: Assistant
dialogue.line: You brought a tennis racket to the office.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's right.

dialogue.speaker: Assistant
dialogue.line: In case you had time to play tennis.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Exactly! You never know when you're gonna have time, so...

jokes.text: All right, all right. Forget it. But you don't have to mention any of this... to Miss Landis, do you? / I don't have to, but I will.
jokes.setup: Elaine hopes assistant won't tell on her
jokes.punchline: Assistant's blunt honesty about tattling
jokes.explanation: Good punchline structure - assistant's honesty about being a tattletale

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You're not going to tell anyone about this, right?

dialogue.speaker: Assistant
dialogue.line: Oh, I'm telling everyone.

jokes.text: Hi, Cosmo. / Hi, Mr. Clotworthy. / How are you today? / Couldn't be better. / Hi, Lorraine. / Hi, Cosmo.
jokes.setup: Kramer enters restaurant as 'Cosmo'
jokes.punchline: Everyone naturally calls him by his real name
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing Kramer's name acceptance - everyone knows him as Cosmo

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Hello, I'm Cosmo.

dialogue.speaker: Restaurant patrons
dialogue.line: Hi Kramer!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No, no, it's Cosmo.

dialogue.speaker: Restaurant patrons
dialogue.line: Sure thing, Kramer.

jokes.text: I don't know the exact pronunciation... but I believe it's 'ménage à trois.'
jokes.setup: Jerry executing his plan with Sandi
jokes.punchline: His awkward, formal pronunciation of ménage à trois
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment - Jerry's awkward formality makes it funnier

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So I said to her, 'You know, we could have a ménage à trois.'

dialogue.speaker: Sandi
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A muh-NAHJ... uh... à... trois.

jokes.text: That is a wild idea.
jokes.setup: Jerry expects disgust at ménage à trois suggestion
jokes.punchline: Sandi's positive reaction calls it 'wild' (good wild)
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection - subverts expectation of disgust with enthusiasm

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So I suggested a ménage à trois.

dialogue.speaker: Sandi
dialogue.line: That's wild!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wild? You're not disgusted?

dialogue.speaker: Sandi
dialogue.line: No, it sounds wild. Good wild.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah. I was sorry to hear she left. / Babs left? / Yeah. She quit today.
jokes.setup: George asking about Babs the matron
jokes.punchline: Learning she quit the day he needed her
jokes.explanation: Good plot complication - Murphy's law timing for George's scheme

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So what happened to Babs? I need her to vouch for me.

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: Oh, she quit.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Quit? When?

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: Today.

jokes.text: What are you doing in here, George?
jokes.setup: George has followed Nina into the women's bathroom
jokes.punchline: Nina discovering him there
jokes.explanation: Peak cringe moment - George's desperation leads to inappropriate behavior

dialogue.speaker: Nina
dialogue.line: What are you doing in here?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I was just... I wanted to talk to you.

dialogue.speaker: Nina
dialogue.line: Get out! Get out of here!

jokes.text: From what I hear, you postmen don't have to look too far. / Well, you know, sometimes it just has a way of finding you.
jokes.setup: Babs making comment about postmen and trouble
jokes.punchline: Newman's suggestive response about trouble finding him
jokes.explanation: Mild flirtation humor between Babs and Newman - character appropriate

dialogue.speaker: Babs
dialogue.line: Postmen always find trouble.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: Trouble? Trouble finds me.

jokes.text: No, actually, she took it pretty well. / So, what happened? / She's into it.
jokes.setup: George expects Jerry got thrown out
jokes.punchline: Jerry reveals Sandi liked the ménage à trois idea
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - plan backfired in unexpected way

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So what happened? Did she throw you out?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, actually she seemed really into the idea.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What? She liked it?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, she was all for it.

jokes.text: And not only that, she just called me. She told me she spoke with the roommate... and the roommate's into the ménage too.
jokes.setup: Jerry's plan has backfired
jokes.punchline: Both women are interested in the threesome
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the backfired plan - problem gets worse

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: This is not how I planned this.

dialogue.speaker: Woman 1
dialogue.line: So you're interested?

dialogue.speaker: Woman 2
dialogue.line: I think it could be fun.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait, what? No, no, no...

jokes.text: Do you ever just get down on your knees and thank God... that you know me and have access to my dementia?
jokes.setup: Jerry created an impossible situation
jokes.punchline: His grandiose self-assessment of his crazy schemes
jokes.explanation: Excellent self-aware humor - Jerry acknowledging his own absurd thought processes

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm like a problem-solving machine. I see a problem, I solve it. I don't even think about it anymore.

jokes.text: What are you talking about? I'm not gonna do it. / You're not gonna do it? What do you mean? / I can't. I'm not an orgy guy.
jokes.setup: George thinks Jerry has amazing opportunity
jokes.punchline: Jerry's matter-of-fact self-assessment
jokes.explanation: 'I'm not an orgy guy' is perfect Jerry self-awareness - highly quotable

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: This is it! This is the opportunity of a lifetime! You're going to be huge!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm not going to be huge.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Because I'm not huge. I'm medium. I've always been medium.

jokes.text: This is like discovering plutonium by accident.
jokes.setup: George can't believe Jerry won't do the ménage à trois
jokes.punchline: His comparison to accidental scientific discovery
jokes.explanation: Brilliant metaphor - comparing sexual opportunity to scientific breakthrough

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You're like Fleming! You're like Alexander Fleming with penicillin. He didn't set out to discover penicillin. He just left a sandwich in a lab and came back and there was penicillin all over it!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's not how it happened.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, that's how it could happen! And that's what you're passing up. You could stumble onto something great!

jokes.text: I'd have to dress and act different. I'd have to grow a mustache and get all kinds of robes and lotions... and get a new bedspread, new curtains. I'd have to get thick carpeting and weirdo lighting. Then I'd have to get new friends. I'd have to get orgy friends.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining why he can't be an orgy guy
jokes.punchline: His detailed list of lifestyle changes required
jokes.explanation: Peak Seinfeld - brilliant escalating list of absurd lifestyle requirements. Highly quotable and original.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I can't be an orgy guy. I'm not built for it. I'd have to make a lot of lifestyle changes. I'd have to get a bigger apartment, for one thing. You need space for an orgy. I'd have to get better furniture, nicer things. I'd have to work out more, eat better. I'd need a better car. Women would expect that. You can't show up to an orgy in a Datsun. And I'd have to grow a mustache. All the orgy guys have mustaches. You don't see a guy without a mustache at an orgy. That's a rule.

jokes.text: Ma! / Cosmo. / I'm sorry. We weren't... We didn't... / Cosmo?
jokes.setup: George finds Kramer's mom with Newman
jokes.punchline: Newman's confused reaction to hearing 'Cosmo'
jokes.explanation: Good callback and awkward situation convergence - Newman learning the name

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What are you doing with Kramer's mother?

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: Kramer's mother? I don't know any Kramer.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Cosmo. Cosmo Kramer.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: Cosmo?

tmdb_overview: George suspects that his girlfriend is bulimic; Jerry dates a woman who has a friendlier roommate; Elaine loans out her boss's tennis racket.
insight: The Switch trades premise consistency for character chaos, landing 60 jokes at middling impact.
